Abstract: Prior approaches for membership privacy preservation usually update or retrain all weights in neural networks, which is costly and can lead to unnecessary utility loss or even more serious misalignment in predictions between training data and non-training data. In this work, we observed three insights: i) privacy vulnerability exists in a very small fraction of weights; ii) however, most of those weights also critically impact utility performance; iii) the importance of weights stems from their locations rather than their values. According to these insights, to preserve privacy, we score critical weights, and instead of discarding those neurons, we rewind only the weights for fine-tuning. We show that, through extensive experiments, this mechanism exhibits outperforming resilience in most cases against Membership Inference Attacks while maintaining utility.

</details>


### [270] [Representation Learning for Spatiotemporal Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.13227)
*Helen Qu,Rudy Morel,Michael McCabe,Alberto Bietti,François Lanusse,Shirley Ho,Yann LeCun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shifts from next-frame prediction in spatiotemporal physical systems to analyzing models’ ability to estimate governing physical parameters, showing that latent-space methods outperform pixel-based methods for downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of current ML approaches to spatiotemporal physical systems, such as training costs and error compounding, and explore their effectiveness in downstream scientific tasks.

Method: The study evaluates general-purpose self-supervised learning methods in capturing physics-grounded representations suitable for estimating physical parameters, focusing on methods like joint embedding predictive architectures (JEPAs).

Result: Self-supervised methods in latent space outperform those using pixel-level prediction in producing useful physical representations for downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Physics-grounded representation learning for scientific tasks benefits from latent-space approaches over traditional pixel-level modeling, challenging assumptions about model designs.

Abstract: Machine learning approaches to spatiotemporal physical systems have primarily focused on next-frame prediction, with the goal of learning an accurate emulator for the system's evolution in time. However, these emulators are computationally expensive to train and are subject to performance pitfalls, such as compounding errors during autoregressive rollout. In this work, we take a different perspective and look at scientific tasks further downstream of predicting the next frame, such as estimation of a system's governing physical parameters. Accuracy on these tasks offers a uniquely quantifiable glimpse into the physical relevance of the representations of these models. We evaluate the effectiveness of general-purpose self-supervised methods in learning physics-grounded representations that are useful for downstream scientific tasks. Surprisingly, we find that not all methods designed for physical modeling outperform generic self-supervised learning methods on these tasks, and methods that learn in the latent space (e.g., joint embedding predictive architectures, or JEPAs) outperform those optimizing pixel-level prediction objectives. Code is available at https://github.com/helenqu/physical-representation-learning.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [271] [GPU-Accelerated Genetic Programming for Symbolic Regression with Beagle Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12292)
*Nathan Haut,Ilya Basin,Marzieh Kianinejad,Ruchika Gupta,Elijah Smith,Zachary Perrico,Wolfgang Banzhaf*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: Beagle software enables GPU-based Genetic Programming for Symbolic Regression, outperforming CPU systems in tested benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage GPU processing capabilities for Genetic Programming tasks, specifically symbolic regression, optimizing computational efficiency and performance.

Method: Implemented a GPU-based framework, Beagle, and benchmarked it against StackGP and PySR CPU systems using the Feynman dataset and two fitness functions.

Result: Beagle showed superior performance compared to CPU-based systems under the same time constraints, using GPU for symbolic regression.

Conclusion: GPU-based frameworks like Beagle are highly effective for Symbolic Regression tasks, providing better throughput and performance compared to traditional CPU methods.

Abstract: Beagle is a new software framework that enables execution of Genetic Programming tasks on the GPU. Currently available for symbolic regression, it processes individuals of the population and fitness cases for training in a way that maximizes throughput on extant GPU platforms. In this contribution, we report on the benchmarking of Beagle on the Feynman Symbolic Regression dataset and compare its performance with a fast CPU system called StackGP and the widely available PySR system under the same wall clock budget. We also report on the use of two different fitness functions, one a point-to-point error function, the other a correlation fitness function. The results demonstrate that the Beagle's GPU-aided Symbolic Regression significantly outperforms leading CPU-based frameworks.

</details>


### [272] [SRAM-Based Compute-in-Memory Accelerator for Linear-decay Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12739)
*Hongyang Shang,Shuai Dong,Yahan Yang,Junyi Yang,Peng Zhou,Arindam Basu*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel SRAM-based Compute-in-Memory (CIM) approach for Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), addressing energy and latency bottlenecks in state updates with linear decay approximation and parallel updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) face performance limits due to the serial update of neuron states, causing significant latency and energy issues compared to their event-driven benefits.

Method: The authors propose replacing exponential decay with a linear decay approximation for membrane updates, reducing computational complexity. Additionally, they design an SRAM-based in-memory architecture for in-place, parallel updates to neuron states.

Result: The method yields 1.1x to 16.7x reductions in energy consumption and improves energy efficiency by 15.9x to 69x, with negligible accuracy loss.

Conclusion: Optimizing state-update dynamics using the proposed linear decay and in-memory parallel approach is critical for achieving highly efficient, scalable, and real-time SNN-based computing systems.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have emerged as a biologically inspired alternative to conventional deep networks, offering event-driven and energy-efficient computation. However, their throughput remains constrained by the serial update of neuron membrane states. While many hardware accelerators and Compute-in-Memory (CIM) architectures efficiently parallelize the synaptic operation (W x I) achieving O(1) complexity for matrix-vector multiplication, the subsequent state update step still requires O(N) time to refresh all neuron membrane potentials. This mismatch makes state update the dominant latency and energy bottleneck in SNN inference. To address this challenge, we propose an SRAM-based CIM for SNN with Linear Decay Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LD-LIF) Neuron that co-optimizes algorithm and hardware. At the algorithmic level, we replace the conventional exponential membrane decay with a linear decay approximation, converting costly multiplications into simple additions while accuracy drops only around 1%. At the architectural level, we introduce an in-memory parallel update scheme that performs in-place decay directly within the SRAM array, eliminating the need for global sequential updates. Evaluated on benchmark SNN workloads, the proposed method achieves a 1.1 x to 16.7 x reduction of SOP energy consumption, while providing 15.9 x to 69 x more energy efficiency, with negligible accuracy loss relative to original decay models. This work highlights that beyond accelerating the (W x I) computation, optimizing state-update dynamics within CIM architectures is essential for scalable, low-power, and real-time neuromorphic processing.

</details>


### [273] [Federated Few-Shot Learning on Neuromorphic Hardware: An Empirical Study Across Physical Edge Nodes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.13037)
*Steven Motta,Gioele Nanni*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: Authors explore federated learning on neuromorphic hardware using BrainChip Akida processors and assess four weight-exchange strategies, with FedUnion outperforming others in preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze the viability of federated learning algorithms on neuromorphic hardware, overcoming challenges with binary weight updates in spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP).

Method: A two-node federated system is implemented using BrainChip Akida AKD1000 processors. Around 1,580 experimental trials over seven analysis phases tested four weight-exchange strategies and their impact on learning accuracy.

Result: Neuron-level concatenation (FedUnion) preserved accuracy, while element-wise averaging (FedAvg) degraded it. Scaling feature dimensions and domain-adaptive fine-tuning improved federated accuracy, achieving a maximum of 77.0% (p < 0.001).

Conclusion: Federated learning on neuromorphic hardware is feasible with appropriate weight-exchange strategies and feature extractor fine-tuning. Prototype complementarity is crucial for effective cross-node transfer.

Abstract: Federated learning on neuromorphic hardware remains unexplored because on-chip spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) produces binary weight updates rather than the floating-point gradients assumed by standard algorithms. We build a two-node federated system with BrainChip Akida AKD1000 processors and run approximately 1,580 experimental trials across seven analysis phases. Of four weight-exchange strategies tested, neuron-level concatenation (FedUnion) consistently preserves accuracy while element-wise weight averaging (FedAvg) destroys it (p = 0.002). Domain-adaptive fine-tuning of the upstream feature extractor accounts for most of the accuracy gains, confirming feature quality as the dominant factor. Scaling feature dimensionality from 64 to 256 yields 77.0% best-strategy federated accuracy (n=30, p < 0.001). Two independent asymmetries (wider features help federation more than individual learning, while binarization hurts federation more) point to a shared prototype complementarity mechanism: cross-node transfer scales with the distinctiveness of neuron prototypes.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [274] [Critical Sections Are Not Per-Thread: A Trace Semantics for Lock-Based Concurrency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.13142)
*Martin Sulzmann*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the limitations of the standard lock set construction by proposing a trace-based characterization of critical sections that spans multiple threads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard lock set methods typically limit critical sections to being confined to individual threads, which does not reflect common behavior in C/Pthread programs.

Method: The research analyzes a trace model for C/Pthread programs and introduces a more comprehensive, trace-based approach to defining critical sections.

Result: It demonstrates that critical sections can naturally span multiple threads, a behavior overlooked by standard lock mechanisms.

Conclusion: Multi-thread critical sections bridge semantic gaps in traditional lock set constructions and reflect the behavior of real-world programs.

Abstract: Locks are a standard mechanism for synchronizing concurrent threads. The standard lock set construction assumes that critical sections are confined to a single thread, and therefore only accounts for locks acquired within that thread. The commonly used notion of a critical section implicitly assumes that protected events belong to the same thread. We show that this assumption is not valid for general C/Pthread executions.
  Using a trace model that captures the essence of C/Pthread programs, we give a trace-based characterization of critical sections that does not impose a per-thread restriction. As a result, critical sections may span multiple threads. Such \emph{multi-thread} critical sections arise naturally in real programs and close a semantic gap in the standard lock set construction.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [275] [A Learning-Based Approach for Contact Detection, Localization, and Force Estimation of Continuum Manipulators With Integrated OFDR Optical Fiber](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12347)
*Mobina Tavangarifard,Jonathan S. Kacines,Qiyu Li,Farshid Alambeigi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a cascade learning-based framework for continuum manipulators equipped with an OFDR fiber to enable contact detection, localization, and force estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenges arise with continuum manipulators in detecting and estimating forces when interactions occur at unknown locations due to their compliant structure and distributed deformation.

Method: A learning-based framework uses a Gradient Boosting classifier for contact detection and a CNN--FiLM model to predict contact location and force distribution based on strain data from OFDR sensors.

Result: The method successfully demonstrated contact detection, localization, and force estimation on a sensorized tendon-driven continuum manipulator in constrained environments.

Conclusion: The proposed framework shows that a single distributed OFDR fiber is sufficient for comprehensive contact inference in continuum manipulators, paving the way for enhancements in minimally invasive procedures.

Abstract: Continuum manipulators (CMs) are widely used in minimally invasive procedures due to their compliant structure and ability to navigate deep and confined anatomical environments. However, their distributed deformation makes force sensing, contact detection, localization, and force estimation challenging, particularly when interactions occur at unknown arc-length locations along the robot. To address this problem, we propose a cascade learning-based framework (CLF) for CMs instrumented with a single distributed Optical Frequency Domain Reflectometry (OFDR) fiber embedded along one side of the robot. The OFDR sensor provides dense strain measurements along the manipulator backbone, capturing strain perturbations caused by external interactions. The proposed CLF first detects contact using a Gradient Boosting classifier and then estimates contact location and interaction force magnitude using a CNN--FiLM model that predicts a spatial force distribution along the manipulator. Experimental validation on a sensorized tendon-driven CM in an obstructed environment demonstrates that a single distributed OFDR fiber provides sufficient information to jointly infer contact occurrence, location, and force in continuum manipulators.

</details>


### [276] [GNN-DIP: Neural Corridor Selection for Decomposition-Based Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12361)
*Peng Xie,Yanlinag Huang,Wenyuan Wu,Amr Alanwar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents GNN-DIP, a framework that addresses motion planning in narrow passages by combining Graph Neural Networks and Decomposition-Informed Planners, significantly improving planning success rates and speed compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenge of motion planning in narrow passages, where most planners fail due to difficulty in sampling and successful path validation in critical narrow regions.

Method: The authors integrate a Graph Neural Network (GNN) with a two-phase Decomposition-Informed Planner (DIP). The GNN predicts portal scores on the cell adjacency graph to bias corridor search, improving efficiency. The method uses CDT with the Funnel algorithm in 2D and slab convex decomposition with portal-face sampling in 3D.

Result: Benchmark tests on different scenarios demonstrate the framework achieves 99–100% success rates and speeds up 2–280 times over traditional sampling-based planners.

Conclusion: GNN-DIP provides a highly efficient and effective approach for navigating narrow passages, outperforming traditional methods in success rates and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Motion planning through narrow passages remains a core challenge: sampling-based planners rarely place samples inside these narrow but critical regions, and even when samples land inside a passage, the straight-line connections between them run close to obstacle boundaries and are frequently rejected by collision checking. Decomposition-based planners resolve both issues by partitioning free space into convex cells -- every passage is captured exactly as a cell boundary, and any path within a cell is collision-free by construction. However, the number of candidate corridors through the cell graph grows combinatorially with environment complexity, creating a bottleneck in corridor selection. We present GNN-DIP, a framework that addresses this by integrating a Graph Neural Network (GNN) with a two-phase Decomposition-Informed Planner (DIP). The GNN predicts portal scores on the cell adjacency graph to bias corridor search toward near-optimal regions while preserving completeness. In 2D, Constrained Delaunay Triangulation (CDT) with the Funnel algorithm yields exact shortest paths within corridors; in 3D, Slab convex decomposition with portal-face sampling provides near-optimal path evaluation. Benchmarks on 2D narrow-passage scenarios, 3D bottleneck environments with up to 246 obstacles, and dynamic 2D settings show that GNN-DIP achieves 99--100% success rates with 2--280 times speedup over sampling-based baselines.

</details>


### [277] [Push, Press, Slide: Mode-Aware Planar Contact Manipulation via Reduced-Order Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12399)
*Melih Özcan,Ozgur S. Oguz,Umut Orguner*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel mode-aware framework simplifying planar manipulation tasks using reduced-order modeling and contact topology selection, enabling efficient and optimization-free robotic control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the challenges in non-prehensile planar manipulation tasks, such as hybrid contact mechanics and complex friction dynamics, which typically require computationally expensive iterative control solutions.

Method: The proposed framework maps contact topologies to reduced-order non-holonomic models and employs an algebraic allocator to compute forces, integrating manipulator kinematics for long-term feasibility without iterative optimization.

Result: The approach is validated in simulations, successfully demonstrating fast and diverse planar manipulation tasks for both single-arm and bimanual robotic systems.

Conclusion: By leveraging reduced-order modeling, contact topology selection, and efficient force allocation, the study presents a practical and computationally efficient method for complex robotic manipulation problems.

Abstract: Non-prehensile planar manipulation, including pushing and press-and-slide, is critical for diverse robotic tasks, but notoriously challenging due to hybrid contact mechanics, under-actuation, and asymmetric friction limits that traditionally necessitate computationally expensive iterative control. In this paper, we propose a mode-aware framework for planar manipulation with one or two robotic arms based on contact topology selection and reduced-order kinematic modeling. Our core insight is that complex wrench-twist limit surface mechanics can be abstracted into a discrete library of physically intuitive models. We systematically map various single-arm and bimanual contact topologies to simple non-holonomic formulations, e.g. unicycle for simplified press-and-slide motion. By anchoring trajectory generation to these reduced-order models, our framework computes the required object wrench and distributes feasible, friction-bounded contact forces via a direct algebraic allocator. We incorporate manipulator kinematics to ensure long-horizon feasibility and demonstrate our fast, optimization-free approach in simulation across diverse single-arm and bimanual manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [278] [Beyond Motion Imitation: Is Human Motion Data Alone Sufficient to Explain Gait Control and Biomechanics?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12408)
*Xinyi Liu,Jangwhan Ahn,Edgar Lobaton,Jennie Si,He Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Incorporating foot-ground interaction measures as rewards in motion imitation learning improves biomechanical accuracy of human gait predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the biomechanical realism of human gait simulations used in biomechanics and wearable robotics.

Method: A reinforcement learning-based imitation learning framework incorporating additional foot-ground interaction measures, such as contact forces, in the reward terms.

Result: Enhanced prediction of joint moments and improved biomechanical consistency, addressing the limitations of kinematics-focused imitation learning approaches.

Conclusion: Kinetics-based reward shaping is essential for achieving physically consistent gait simulations in human biomechanics and wearable robotics research.

Abstract: With the growing interest in motion imitation learning (IL) for human biomechanics and wearable robotics, this study investigates how additional foot-ground interaction measures, used as reward terms, affect human gait kinematics and kinetics estimation within a reinforcement learning-based IL framework. Results indicate that accurate reproduction of forward kinematics alone does not ensure biomechanically plausible joint kinetics. Adding foot-ground contacts and contact forces to the IL reward terms enables the prediction of joint moments in forward walking simulation, which are significantly closer to those computed by inverse dynamics. This finding highlights a fundamental limitation of motion-only IL approaches, which may prioritize kinematics matching over physical consistency. Incorporating kinetic constraints, particularly ground reaction force and center of pressure information, significantly enhances the realism of internal and external kinetics. These findings suggest that, when imitation learning is applied to human-related research domains such as biomechanics and wearable robot co-design, kinetics-based reward shaping is necessary to achieve physically consistent gait representations.

</details>


### [279] [Predictive and adaptive maps for long-term visual navigation in changing environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12460)
*Lucie Halodova,Eliska Dvorakova,Filip Majer,Tomas Vintr,Oscar Martinez Mozos,Feras Dayoub,Tomas Krajnik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper evaluates map management strategies for long-term visual navigation in changing environments, focusing on feature selection, removal, and update to maintain localization accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve robot long-term navigation by addressing challenges posed by environmental appearance changes that affect map reliability.

Method: The authors propose several map management strategies, including approaches that model cyclic environmental changes and predict feature visibility based on time and location.

Result: The cyclic modeling strategies demonstrated better robot localization accuracy compared to non-temporal approaches after conducting three-month experiments.

Conclusion: Incorporating temporal models of environmental changes significantly enhances navigation system performance in changing environments.

Abstract: In this paper, we compare different map management techniques for long-term visual navigation in changing environments. In this scenario, the navigation system needs to continuously update and refine its feature map in order to adapt to the environment appearance change. To achieve reliable long-term navigation, the map management techniques have to (i) select features useful for the current navigation task, (ii) remove features that are obsolete, (iii) and add new features from the current camera view to the map. We propose several map management strategies and evaluate their performance with regard to the robot localisation accuracy in long-term teach-and-repeat navigation. Our experiments, performed over three months, indicate that strategies which model cyclic changes of the environment appearance and predict which features are going to be visible at a particular time and location, outperform strategies which do not explicitly model the temporal evolution of the changes.

</details>


### [280] [One-Step Flow Policy: Self-Distillation for Fast Visuomotor Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12480)
*Shaolong Li,Lichao Sun,Yongchao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces the One-Step Flow Policy (OFP), optimizing robot control with high accuracy and significantly reducing inference latency compared to generative models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative robotic policies rely on iterative sampling, which causes delays during manipulation tasks. The motivation is to create a high-fidelity policy for real-time, precise robotic control.

Method: The paper proposes OFP, a self-distillation framework for single-step action generation. It includes self-consistency loss, self-guided regularization, and a warm-start mechanism to ensure coherent action transport and minimize latency.

Result: OFP achieves state-of-the-art results in 56 simulated manipulation tasks, outperforms 100-step generative models, and speeds up action generation by over 100 times. It also surpasses previous policies in RoboTwin 2.0 integration.

Conclusion: OFP presents a practical, scalable approach to robot control, combining precision and efficiency suitable for time-sensitive applications.

Abstract: Generative flow and diffusion models provide the continuous, multimodal action distributions needed for high-precision robotic policies. However, their reliance on iterative sampling introduces severe inference latency, degrading control frequency and harming performance in time-sensitive manipulation. To address this problem, we propose the One-Step Flow Policy (OFP), a from-scratch self-distillation framework for high-fidelity, single-step action generation without a pre-trained teacher. OFP unifies a self-consistency loss to enforce coherent transport across time intervals, and a self-guided regularization to sharpen predictions toward high-density expert modes. In addition, a warm-start mechanism leverages temporal action correlations to minimize the generative transport distance. Evaluations across 56 diverse simulated manipulation tasks demonstrate that a one-step OFP achieves state-of-the-art results, outperforming 100-step diffusion and flow policies while accelerating action generation by over $100\times$. We further integrate OFP into the $π_{0.5}$ model on RoboTwin 2.0, where one-step OFP surpasses the original 10-step policy. These results establish OFP as a practical, scalable solution for highly accurate and low-latency robot control.

</details>


### [281] [COAD: Constant-Time Planning for Continuous Goal Manipulation with Compressed Library and Online Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12488)
*Adil Shiyas,Zhuoyun Zhong,Constantinos Chamzas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents COAD, a framework for constant-time robotic motion planning across continuous task spaces using compressed libraries and fast adaptation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots often face repetitive motion-planning problems in environments with fixed workspaces but varying goal object positions. Current solutions are inefficient, requiring large libraries and lack continuous task space guarantees.

Method: COAD discretizes the task space, compresses the motion library using representative root problems, and employs lightweight adaptation methods for fast query handling.

Result: The framework achieves substantial motion library compression, high success rates, and sub-millisecond query times in simulations and real-world evaluations. It outperforms existing methods in efficiency and path quality.

Conclusion: COAD provides an efficient and reliable motion-planning solution for robots, addressing the need for faster and less memory-intensive methods in continuous task spaces.

Abstract: In many robotic manipulation tasks, the robot repeatedly solves motion-planning problems that differ mainly in the location of the goal object and its associated obstacle, while the surrounding workspace remains fixed. Prior works have shown that leveraging experience and offline computation can accelerate repeated planning queries, but they lack guarantees of covering the continuous task space and require storing large libraries of solutions. In this work, we present COAD, a framework that provides constant-time planning over a continuous goal-parameterized task space. COAD discretizes the continuous task space into finitely many Task Coverage Regions. Instead of planning and storing solutions for every region offline, it constructs a compressed library by only solving representative root problems. Other problems are handled through fast adaptation from these root solutions. At query time, the system retrieves a root motion in constant time and adapts it to the desired goal using lightweight adaptation modules such as linear interpolation, Dynamic Movement Primitives, or simple trajectory optimization. We evaluate the framework on various manipulators and environments in simulation and the real world, showing that COAD achieves substantial compression of the motion library while maintaining high success rates and sub-millisecond-level queries, outperforming baseline methods in both efficiency and path quality. The source code is available at https://github.com/elpis-lab/CoAd.

</details>


### [282] [Robots that redesign themselves through kinematic self-destruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12505)
*Chen Yu,Sam Kriegman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a robot capable of dynamically redesigning its own body through self-destruction to improve locomotion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the concept that robots can achieve enhanced adaptability and efficiency by participating actively in their own design.

Method: Using proprioceptive feedback and an autoregressive sequence model, the robot identifies and removes redundant body parts to enhance its motion.

Result: The robot demonstrates improved forward locomotion after self-design, performing better than random link removals or fixed designs.

Conclusion: Self-designing robots show potential advantages over predesigned counterparts by enhancing adaptability and effectiveness in certain contexts.

Abstract: Every robot built to date was predesigned by an external process, prior to deployment. Here we show a robot that actively participates in its own design during its lifetime. Starting from a randomly assembled body, and using only proprioceptive feedback, the robot dynamically ``sculpts'' itself into a new design through kinematic self-destruction: identifying redundant links within its body that inhibit its locomotion, and then thrashing those links against the surface until they break at the joint and fall off the body. It does so using a single autoregressive sequence model, a universal controller that learns in simulation when and how to simplify a robot's body through self-destruction and then adaptively controls the reduced morphology. The optimized policy successfully transfers to reality and generalizes to previously unseen kinematic trees, generating forward locomotion that is more effective than otherwise equivalent policies that randomly remove links or cannot remove any. This suggests that self-designing robots may be more successful than predesigned robots in some cases, and that kinematic self-destruction, though reductive and irreversible, could provide a general adaptive strategy for a wide range of robots.

</details>


### [283] [Red-Teaming Vision-Language-Action Models via Quality Diversity Prompt Generation for Robust Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12510)
*Siddharth Srikanth,Freddie Liang,Sophie Hsu,Varun Bhatt,Shihan Zhao,Henry Chen,Bryon Tjanaka,Minjune Hwang,Akanksha Saran,Daniel Seita,Aaquib Tabrez,Stefanos Nikolaidis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces Q-DIG, a framework aimed at improving robustness in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by generating diverse, task-relevant adversarial instructions to identify vulnerabilities and improve task success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the sensitivity of VLA-based robots to variations in language instructions and the difficulty in predicting when they might fail.

Method: Q-DIG combines Quality Diversity techniques with Vision-Language Models to scalably generate a variety of adversarial instructions that remain task-relevant while exposing weaknesses in the VLA systems.

Result: The approach discovered more diverse and meaningful failure modes than baseline methods, improved task success rates through fine-tuning, and was validated through simulation, user studies, and real-world tests.

Conclusion: Q-DIG effectively identifies vulnerabilities in VLA systems, enhances robustness, and shows promise in improving the performance of VLA-based robotic systems.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have significant potential to enable general-purpose robotic systems for a range of vision-language tasks. However, the performance of VLA-based robots is highly sensitive to the precise wording of language instructions, and it remains difficult to predict when such robots will fail. To improve the robustness of VLAs to different wordings, we present Q-DIG (Quality Diversity for Diverse Instruction Generation), which performs red-teaming by scalably identifying diverse natural language task descriptions that induce failures while remaining task-relevant. Q-DIG integrates Quality Diversity (QD) techniques with Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to generate a broad spectrum of adversarial instructions that expose meaningful vulnerabilities in VLA behavior. Our results across multiple simulation benchmarks show that Q-DIG finds more diverse and meaningful failure modes compared to baseline methods, and that fine-tuning VLAs on the generated instructions improves task success rates. Furthermore, results from a user study highlight that Q-DIG generates prompts judged to be more natural and human-like than those from baselines. Finally, real-world evaluations of Q-DIG prompts show results consistent with simulation, and fine-tuning VLAs on the generated prompts further success rates on unseen instructions. Together, these findings suggest that Q-DIG is a promising approach for identifying vulnerabilities and improving the robustness of VLA-based robots. Our anonymous project website is at qdigvla.github.io.

</details>


### [284] [Beyond Dense Futures: World Models as Structured Planners for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12553)
*Minghao Jin,Mozheng Liao,Mingfei Han,Zhihui Li,Xiaojun Chang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: StructVLA improves robotic manipulation by reformulating world models into explicit, structured planners, effectively addressing issues with dense rollout errors and weak alignment in sparse methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies and errors in current prediction-based and sparse high-level planning methods for robotic manipulation, aligning long-horizon planning with low-level execution.

Method: The approach uses structured frames derived from intrinsic kinematic cues as sparse spatiotemporal milestones. These are trained in a two-stage paradigm: first predicting these structured frames, then mapping them into low-level actions through a unified token vocabulary.

Result: StructVLA achieves high average success rates (75.0% on SimplerEnv-WidowX, 94.8% on LIBERO), and real-world deployment confirms its task completion and generalization capability.

Conclusion: StructVLA successfully bridges visual planning and motion control, improving both reliability and generalization in robotic tasks by combining sparse structure with kinematic grounding.

Abstract: Recent world-model-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures have improved robotic manipulation through predictive visual foresight. However, dense future prediction introduces visual redundancy and accumulates errors, causing long-horizon plan drift. Meanwhile, recent sparse methods typically represent visual foresight using high-level semantic subtasks or implicit latent states. These representations often lack explicit kinematic grounding, weakening the alignment between planning and low-level execution. To address this, we propose StructVLA, which reformulates a generative world model into an explicit structured planner for reliable control. Instead of dense rollouts or semantic goals, StructVLA predicts sparse, physically meaningful structured frames. Derived from intrinsic kinematic cues (e.g., gripper transitions and kinematic turning points), these frames capture spatiotemporal milestones closely aligned with task progress. We implement this approach through a two-stage training paradigm with a unified discrete token vocabulary: the world model is first trained to predict structured frames and subsequently optimized to map the structured foresight into low-level actions. This approach provides clear physical guidance and bridges visual planning and motion control. In our experiments, StructVLA achieves strong average success rates of 75.0% on SimplerEnv-WidowX and 94.8% on LIBERO. Real-world deployments further demonstrate reliable task completion and robust generalization across both basic pick-and-place and complex long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [285] [From Woofs to Words: Towards Intelligent Robotic Guide Dogs with Verbal Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12574)
*Yohei Hayamizu,David DeFazio,Hrudayangam Mehta,Zainab Altaweel,Jacqueline Choe,Chao Lin,Jake Juettner,Furui Xiao,Jeremy Blackburn,Shiqi Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a dialog system for robotic guide dogs using LLMs to enhance obstacle avoidance and navigation for visually impaired individuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robotic guide dogs lack effective verbal communication capabilities necessary for collaborative decision-making in dynamic environments.

Method: The authors developed a novel dialog system powered by LLMs to verbalize navigational plans and scenes, enabling improved human-robot interaction.

Result: Experiments included a human study to compare verbalization strategies and a simulation study to evaluate navigation efficiency and accuracy.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the importance of language-enabled robotic guide dogs for enhancing navigation and collaboration for visually impaired users.

Abstract: Assistive robotics is an important subarea of robotics that focuses on the well-being of people with disabilities. A robotic guide dog is an assistive quadruped robot that helps visually impaired people in obstacle avoidance and navigation. Enabling language capabilities for robotic guide dogs goes beyond naively adding an existing dialog system onto a mobile robot. The novel challenges include grounding language in the dynamically changing environment and improving spatial awareness for the human handler. To address those challenges, we develop a novel dialog system for robotic guide dogs that uses LLMs to verbalize both navigational plans and scenes. The goal is to enable verbal communication for collaborative decision-making within the handler-robot team. In experiments, we conducted a human study to evaluate different verbalization strategies and a simulation study to assess the efficiency and accuracy in navigation tasks.

</details>


### [286] [Skill-informed Data-driven Haptic Nudges for High-dimensional Human Motor Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12583)
*Ankur Kamboj,Rajiv Ranganathan,Xiaobo Tan,Vaibhav Srivastava*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduced a framework for optimal haptic nudge feedback in motor learning tasks using predictive models and POMDPs, which was verified through a human-subject experiment showing improved learning outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study seeks to enhance human motor learning in high-dimensional tasks by leveraging data-driven methods to design feedback mechanisms that adapt to the learner's evolving skills.

Method: The stochastic dynamics of motor learning are modeled using an Input-Output Hidden Markov Model, and the feedback design is formulated as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process for deriving optimal guidance policies.

Result: Human participants trained with the POMDP-derived feedback policy demonstrated faster task performance improvements and discovered efficient low-dimensional motor patterns more rapidly compared to alternative feedback approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed data-driven, POMDP-based haptic feedback system effectively accelerates motor learning and reduces dimensional complexity, showcasing its potential in skill training applications.

Abstract: In this work, we propose a data-driven skill-informed framework to design optimal haptic nudge feedback for high-dimensional novel motor learning tasks. We first model the stochastic dynamics of human motor learning using an Input-Output Hidden Markov Model (IOHMM), which explicitly decouples latent skill evolution from observable kinematic emissions. Leveraging this predictive model, we formulate the haptic nudge feedback design problem as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). This allows us to derive an optimal nudging policy that minimizes long-term performance cost, implicitly guiding the learner toward robust regions of the skill space. We validated our approach through a human-subject study ($N=30$) using a high-dimensional hand-exoskeleton task. Results demonstrate that participants trained with the POMDP-derived policy exhibited significantly accelerated task performance compared to groups receiving heuristic-based feedback or no feedback. Furthermore, synergy analysis revealed that the POMDP group discovered efficient low-dimensional motor representations more rapidly.

</details>


### [287] [CarPLAN: Context-Adaptive and Robust Planning with Dynamic Scene Awareness for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12607)
*Junyong Yun,Jungho Kim,ByungHyun Lee,Dongyoung Lee,Sehwan Choi,Seunghyeop Nam,Kichun Jo,Jun Won Choi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces CarPLAN, an imitation learning framework for autonomous vehicle motion planning, emphasizing context understanding and adaptive decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve motion planning in autonomous driving by enhancing driving context understanding and enabling adaptive decision-making using real-world data.

Method: The paper proposes CarPLAN with two main components: 1. Displacement-Aware Predictive Encoding to predict spatial relations for better trajectory generation. 2. Context-Adaptive Multi-Expert Decoder to leverage scene-adaptive expert decoders for dynamic environments.

Result: CarPLAN achieved state-of-the-art performance on the nuPlan benchmark, particularly excelling in challenging scenarios, and showed strong generalization on the Waymax benchmark.

Conclusion: CarPLAN advances IL-based motion planning by combining improved spatial awareness and context adaptability, ensuring robust planning in diverse traffic conditions.

Abstract: Imitation learning (IL) is widely used for motion planning in autonomous driving due to its data efficiency and access to real-world driving data. For safe and robust real-world driving, IL-based planning requires capturing the complex driving contexts inherent in real-world data and enabling context-adaptive decision-making, rather than relying solely on expert trajectory imitation. In this paper, we propose CarPLAN, a novel IL-based motion planning framework that explicitly enhances driving context understanding and enables adaptive planning across diverse traffic scenarios. Our contributions are twofold: We introduce Displacement-Aware Predictive Encoding (DPE) to improve the model's spatial awareness by predicting future displacement vectors between the Autonomous Vehicle (AV) and surrounding scene elements. This allows the planner to account for relational spacing when generating trajectories. In addition to the standard imitation loss, we incorporate an augmented loss term that captures displacement prediction errors, ensuring planning decisions consider relative distances from other agents. To improve the model's ability to handle diverse driving contexts, we propose Context-Adaptive Multi-Expert Decoder (CMD), which leverages the Mixture of Experts (MoE) framework. CMD dynamically selects the most suitable expert decoders based on scene structure at each Transformer layer, enabling adaptive and context-aware planning in dynamic environments. We evaluate CarPLAN on the nuPlan benchmark and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all closed-loop simulation metrics. In particular, CarPLAN exhibits robust performance on challenging scenarios such as Test14-Hard, validating its effectiveness in complex driving conditions. Additional experiments on the Waymax benchmark further demonstrate its generalization capability across different benchmark settings.

</details>


### [288] [Autonomous Integration and Improvement of Robotic Assembly using Skill Graph Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12649)
*Peiqi Yu,Philip Huang,Chaitanya Chawla,Guanya Shi,Jiaoyang Li,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework for autonomous robotic assembly systems using Skill Graph representations to improve task integration, adaptability, and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic assembly systems require significant manual engineering to adapt to changes and improve performance, creating a need for autonomous, scalable solutions.

Method: The authors propose Skill Graphs that organize robot capabilities with semantic descriptions and executable components, enabling integration, planning, and iterative skill refinement.

Result: The framework unifies robotic system configuration, execution, evaluation, and continuous learning under a single structure, improving adaptability and efficiency.

Conclusion: Skill Graphs offer a scalable approach for enhancing robotic assembly systems through autonomous integration, semantic-level planning, and iterative improvement.

Abstract: Robotic assembly systems traditionally require substantial manual engineering effort to integrate new tasks, adapt to new environments, and improve performance over time. This paper presents a framework for autonomous integration and continuous improvement of robotic assembly systems based on Skill Graph representations. A Skill Graph organizes robot capabilities as verb-based skills, explicitly linking semantic descriptions (verbs and nouns) with executable policies, pre-conditions, post-conditions, and evaluators. We show how Skill Graphs enable rapid system integration by supporting semantic-level planning over skills, while simultaneously grounding execution through well-defined interfaces to robot controllers and perception modules. After initial deployment, the same Skill Graph structure supports systematic data collection and closed-loop performance improvement, enabling iterative refinement of skills and their composition. We demonstrate how this approach unifies system configuration, execution, evaluation, and learning within a single representation, providing a scalable pathway toward adaptive and reusable robotic assembly systems. The code is at https://github.com/intelligent-control-lab/AIDF.

</details>


### [289] [TacVLA: Contact-Aware Tactile Fusion for Robust Vision-Language-Action Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12665)
*Kaidi Zhang,Heng Zhang,Zhengtong Xu,Zhiyuan Zhang,Md Rakibul Islam Prince,Xiang Li,Xiaojing Han,Yuhao Zhou,Arash Ajoudani,Yu She*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces TacVLA, a Vision-Language-Action model integrated with tactile feedback to improve robotic manipulation under challenging conditions like occlusion and fine-grained tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of existing VLA models in handling visual occlusion, detailed manipulation tasks, and scenarios requiring physical contact.

Method: They developed a transformer-based TacVLA incorporating tactile modalities and a contact-aware gating mechanism to selectively activate tactile inputs during physical interactions.

Result: TacVLA outperformed baselines with a 20% higher success rate in constraint-locked disassembly, 60% improvement in in-box picking, and 2.1x better performance in visually occluded environments.

Conclusion: TacVLA enhances fine-grained manipulation through adaptive multimodal fusion, demonstrating superior performance in robotic manipulation tasks involving physical interaction.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated significant advantages in robotic manipulation. However, their reliance on vision and language often leads to suboptimal performance in tasks involving visual occlusion, fine-grained manipulation, and physical contact. To address these challenges, we propose TacVLA, a fine-tuned VLA model by incorporating tactile modalities into the transformer-based policy to enhance fine-grained manipulation capabilities. Specifically, we introduce a contact-aware gating mechanism that selectively activates tactile tokens only when contact is detected, enabling adaptive multimodal fusion while avoiding irrelevant tactile interference. The fused visual, language, and tactile tokens are jointly processed within the transformer architecture to strengthen cross-modal grounding during contact-rich interaction. Extensive experiments on constraint-locked disassembly, in-box picking and robustness evaluations demonstrate that our model outperforms baselines, improving the performance by averaging 20% success rate in disassembly, 60% in in-box picking and 2.1x improvement in scenarios with visual occlusion. Videos are available at https://sites.google.com/view/tacvla and code will be released.

</details>


### [290] [Learning Athletic Humanoid Tennis Skills from Imperfect Human Motion Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12686)
*Zhikai Zhang,Haofei Lu,Yunrui Lian,Ziqing Chen,Yun Liu,Chenghuai Lin,Han Xue,Zicheng Zeng,Zekun Qi,Shaolin Zheng,Qing Luan,Jingbo Wang,Junliang Xing,He Wang,Li Yi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces LATENT, a system enabling humanoid robots to learn tennis skills from imperfect human motion data for dynamic gameplay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots struggle to replicate human tennis skills due to the lack of complete and realistic human motion data.

Method: LATENT utilizes fragmented motion data capturing primitive tennis skills to train humanoid robots for dynamic gameplay via correction, composition, and robust sim-to-real policy transfer.

Result: The developed policy allows robots to engage in multi-shot tennis rallies and return balls with precision in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: LATENT demonstrates the potential of learning athletic skills from fragmented data, enabling humanoid robots to perform dynamic sports tasks.

Abstract: Human athletes demonstrate versatile and highly-dynamic tennis skills to successfully conduct competitive rallies with a high-speed tennis ball. However, reproducing such behaviors on humanoid robots is difficult, partially due to the lack of perfect humanoid action data or human kinematic motion data in tennis scenarios as reference. In this work, we propose LATENT, a system that Learns Athletic humanoid TEnnis skills from imperfect human motioN daTa. The imperfect human motion data consist only of motion fragments that capture the primitive skills used when playing tennis rather than precise and complete human-tennis motion sequences from real-world tennis matches, thereby significantly reducing the difficulty of data collection. Our key insight is that, despite being imperfect, such quasi-realistic data still provide priors about human primitive skills in tennis scenarios. With further correction and composition, we learn a humanoid policy that can consistently strike incoming balls under a wide range of conditions and return them to target locations, while preserving natural motion styles. We also propose a series of designs for robust sim-to-real transfer and deploy our policy on the Unitree G1 humanoid robot. Our method achieves surprising results in the real world and can stably sustain multi-shot rallies with human players. Project page: https://zzk273.github.io/LATENT/

</details>


### [291] [HaltNav: Reactive Visual Halting over Lightweight Topological Priors for Robust Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12696)
*Pingcong Li,Zihui Yu,Bichi Zhang,Sören Schwertfeger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents HaltNav, a hierarchical framework for Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN), integrating lightweight text-based maps (osmAG) with local exploration and robust global planning. It improves navigation in dynamic environments by detecting and adapting to changes like obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance Vision-and-Language Navigation by moving away from complex metric maps toward lightweight, practical, text-based representations, while addressing real-world challenges like environmental changes that affect navigation paths.

Method: The authors propose HaltNav, which couples global planning using text-based osmAG with local exploration using an MLLM-based brain module. A mechanism called Reactive Visual Halting (RVH) detects and responds to obstacles by updating the osmAG and replanning navigation routes.

Result: HaltNav outperforms baseline models by demonstrating higher robustness in navigation tasks over long distances and under dynamic environmental conditions. This is achieved without relying on exhaustive language instructions.

Conclusion: The approach leverages lightweight topological maps combined with hierarchical navigation strategies to improve efficiency and adaptability in real-world VLN scenarios, making it robust to unpredictable environmental changes.

Abstract: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) is shifting from rigid, step-by-step instruction following toward open-vocabulary, goal-oriented autonomy. Achieving this transition without exhaustive routing prompts requires agents to leverage structural priors. While prior work often assumes computationally heavy 2D/3D metric maps, we instead exploit a lightweight, text-based osmAG (OpenStreetMap Area Graph), a floorplan-level topological representation that is easy to obtain and maintain. However, global planning over a prior map alone is brittle in real-world deployments, where local connectivity can change (e.g., closed doors or crowded passages), leading to execution-time failures. To address this gap, we propose a hierarchical navigation framework HaltNav that couples the robust global planning of osmAG with the local exploration and instruction-grounding capability of VLN. Our approach features an MLLM-based brain module, which is capable of high-level task grounding and obstruction awareness. Conditioned on osmAG, the brain converts the global route into a sequence of localized execution snippets, providing the VLN executor with prior-grounded, goal-centric sub-instructions. Meanwhile, it detects local anomalies via a mechanism we term Reactive Visual Halting (RVH), which interrupts the local control loop, updates osmAG by invalidating the corresponding topology, and triggers replanning to orchestrate a viable detour. To train this halting capability efficiently, we introduce a data synthesis pipeline that leverages generative models to inject realistic obstacles into otherwise navigable scenes, substantially enriching hard negative samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our hierarchical framework outperforms several baseline methods without tedious language instructions, and significantly improves robustness for long-horizon vision-language navigation under environmental changes.

</details>


### [292] [Altered Thoughts, Altered Actions: Probing Chain-of-Thought Vulnerabilities in VLA Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12717)
*Tuan Duong Trinh,Naveed Akhtar,Basim Azam*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study investigates how the intermediate reasoning trace in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models can be adversarially corrupted to affect robot task performance, revealing entity-reference reliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the vulnerability of intermediate reasoning traces in VLA models and assess their impact on task performance when adversarially corrupted.

Method: Designed seven corruption methods categorized into three tiers of attackers and tested them on a state-of-the-art reasoning VLA model across 40 LIBERO manipulation tasks to study performance degradation.

Result: Entity name substitution significantly impacted task performance, reducing success rates by up to 45 percentage points for certain tasks, while other corruption methods had negligible effects.

Conclusion: Entity-referring text integrity is crucial for action decoders in reasoning-based VLAs, exposing this trace as a novel vulnerability that input-validation defenses cannot detect.

Abstract: Recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models increasingly adopt chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, generating a natural-language plan before decoding motor commands. This internal text channel between the reasoning module and the action decoder has received no adversarial scrutiny. We ask: which properties of this intermediate plan does the action decoder actually rely on, and can targeted corruption of the reasoning trace alone -- with all inputs left intact -- degrade a robot's physical task performance? We design a taxonomy of seven text corruptions organized into three attacker tiers (blind noise, mechanical-semantic, and LLM-adaptive) and apply them to a state-of-the-art reasoning VLA across 40 LIBERO tabletop manipulation tasks. Our results reveal a striking asymmetry: substituting object names in the reasoning trace reduces overall success rate by 8.3~percentage points (pp) -- reaching $-$19.3~pp on goal-conditioned tasks and $-$45~pp on individual tasks -- whereas sentence reordering, spatial-direction reversal, token noise, and even a 70B-parameter LLM crafting plausible-but-wrong plans all have negligible impact (within $\pm$4~pp). This asymmetry indicates that the action decoder depends on entity-reference integrity rather than reasoning quality or sequential structure. Notably, a sophisticated LLM-based attacker underperforms simple mechanical object-name substitution, because preserving plausibility inadvertently retains the entity-grounding structure the decoder needs. A cross-architecture control using a non-reasoning VLA confirms the vulnerability is exclusive to reasoning-augmented models, while instruction-level attacks degrade both architectures -- establishing that the internal reasoning trace is a distinct and stealthy threat vector invisible to input-validation defenses.

</details>


### [293] [AnchorVLA4D: an Anchor-Based Spatial-Temporal Vision-Language-Action Model for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12730)
*Juan Zhu,Zhanying Shao,Xiaoqi Li,Ethan Morgan,Jiadong Xu,Hongwei Fan,Hao Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces AnchorVLA4D, a new vision-language-action (VLA) model that incorporates spatial and temporal reasoning by using an anchor image and spatial encoder for robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA systems lack detailed spatial perception and memory, resulting in poor handling of object occlusion and spatial disorientation during robotic manipulation.

Method: AnchorVLA4D introduces an anchor image to preserve the initial scene context and integrates a spatial encoder to process relationships between the anchor and current frames, using a Qwen2.5-VL backbone and diffusion-based action head.

Result: AnchorVLA4D achieved a 13.6% performance improvement on the Simpler WidowX benchmark and an 80% average success rate on real-world tasks.

Conclusion: This approach successfully enhances spatial-temporal reasoning in VLAs without requiring additional sensing hardware and with minimal inference overhead.

Abstract: Since current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) systems suffer from limited spatial perception and the absence of memory throughout manipulation, we investigate visual anchors as a means to enhance spatial and temporal reasoning within VLA policies for robotic manipulation. Conventional VLAs generate actions by conditioning on a single current frame together with a language instruction. However, since the frame is encoded as a 2D image, it does not contain detailed spatial information, and the VLA similarly lacks any means to incorporate past context. As a result, it frequently forgets objects under occlusion and becomes spatially disoriented during the manipulation process. Thus, we propose AnchorVLA4D, a simple spatial-temporal VLA that augments the visual input with an anchor image to preserve the initial scene context throughout execution, and adds a lightweight spatial encoder that jointly processes the anchor and current frames to expose geometric relationships within an episode. Built on a Qwen2.5-VL backbone with a diffusion-based action head, AnchorVLA4D requires no additional sensing modalities (e.g., depth or point clouds) and introduces negligible inference overhead. Combining anchoring with a frozen pretrained spatial encoder yields further gains, realizing a 13.6% improvement on the Simpler WidowX benchmark and confirming the approach on real-world tasks, where it achieved an average success rate of 80%.

</details>


### [294] [Conflict Mitigation in Shared Environments using Flow-Aware Multi-Agent Path Finding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12736)
*Lukas Heuer,Yufei Zhu,Luigi Palmieri,Andrey Rudenko,Anna Mannucci,Sven Koenig,Martin Magnusson*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study introduces Flow-Aware Multi-Agent Path Finding (FA-MAPF), which incorporates learned motion patterns into MAPF to handle dynamic environments more effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of deploying large multi-robot systems in shared environments where dynamic, uncontrollable agents can cause operational delays, aiming to improve solution quality in such scenarios.

Method: The authors propose FA-MAPF, a framework that integrates motion pattern data of uncontrollable agents into centralized MAPF algorithms.

Result: The evaluation on diverse maps and a real-world scenario showed FA-MAPF reduces conflicts with uncontrollable agents by up to 55%, maintaining task efficiency.

Conclusion: FA-MAPF enhances MAPF solutions in dynamic environments by effectively handling interaction with uncontrollable agents through motion pattern integration.

Abstract: Deploying multi-robot systems in environments shared with dynamic and uncontrollable agents presents significant challenges, especially for large robot fleets. In such environments, individual robot operations can be delayed due to unforeseen conflicts with uncontrollable agents. While existing research primarily focuses on preserving the completeness of Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) solutions considering delays, there is limited emphasis on utilizing additional environmental information to enhance solution quality in the presence of other dynamic agents. To this end, we propose Flow-Aware Multi-Agent Path Finding (FA-MAPF), a novel framework that integrates learned motion patterns of uncontrollable agents into centralized MAPF algorithms. Our evaluation, conducted on a diverse set of benchmark maps with simulated uncontrollable agents and on a real-world map with recorded human trajectories, demonstrates the effectiveness of FA-MAPF compared to state-of-the-art baselines. The experimental results show that FA-MAPF can consistently reduce conflicts with uncontrollable agents, up to 55%, without compromising task efficiency.

</details>


### [295] [Easy-IIL: Reducing Human Operational Burden in Interactive Imitation Learning via Assistant Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12769)
*Chengjie Zhang,Chao Tang,Wenlong Dong,Dehao Huang,Aoxiang Gu,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Easy-IIL is a framework designed to reduce human involvement in Interactive Imitation Learning (IIL) by combining model-based imitation methods and selective human intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the high demand for human involvement in IIL processes, particularly in active operation. It seeks to leverage the efficiency of structured model-based approaches while minimizing human effort.

Method: The framework uses off-the-shelf model-based imitation methods as an assistant expert. Human experts only provide one demonstration to initialize the assistant and intervene in critical task failure scenarios. Quality data is preserved to maintain performance.

Result: Easy-IIL significantly reduces the human operational burden and maintains comparable performance to mainstream IIL methods in both simulation and real-world experiments. User studies confirm lower subjective workload.

Conclusion: Easy-IIL bridges the gap between reducing human effort and maintaining IIL performance by combining model-based methods with strategic human intervention, making it practical for real-world applications.

Abstract: Interactive Imitation Learning (IIL) typically relies on extensive human involvement for both offline demonstration and online interaction. Prior work primarily focuses on reducing human effort in passive monitoring rather than active operation. Interestingly, structured model-based imitation approaches achieve comparable performance with significantly fewer demonstrations than end-to-end imitation learning policies in the low-data regime. However, these methods are typically surpassed by end-to-end policies as the data increases. Leveraging this insight, we propose Easy-IIL, a framework that utilizes off-the-shelf model-based imitation methods as an assistant expert to replace active human operation for the majority of data collection. The human expert only provides a single demonstration to initialize the assistant expert and intervenes in critical states where the task is approaching failure. Furthermore, Easy-IIL can maintain IIL performance by preserving both offline and online data quality. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that Easy-IIL significantly reduces human operational burden while maintaining performance comparable to mainstream IIL baselines. User studies further confirm that Easy-IIL reduces subjective workload on the human expert. Project page: https://sites.google.com/view/easy-iil

</details>


### [296] [Motion-Specific Battery Health Assessment for Quadrotors Using High-Fidelity Battery Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12791)
*Joonhee Kim,Sanghyun Park,Donghyeong Kim,Eunseon Choi,Soohee Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper discusses a framework for motion-aware battery health assessment in quadrotors, emphasizing that different flight motions can uniquely drive battery degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses a gap in energy management for quadrotors by incorporating a detailed analysis of how flight motions affect battery degradation, often overlooked in traditional methods.

Method: The approach includes a current sensing module to capture real flight current profiles, high-fidelity battery model calibration, and simulations of flight loads with degradation-coupled modeling.

Result: Even when two flight profiles consume the same average energy, they result in different degradation pathways due to transient load structures, showcasing the importance of accounting for motion-induced factors.

Conclusion: Motion-aware battery management is necessary to better balance efficiency and degradation in quadrotors, highlighting the limitations of traditional battery usage assumptions.

Abstract: Quadrotor endurance is ultimately limited by battery behavior, yet most energy aware planning treats the battery as a simple energy reservoir and overlooks how flight motions induce dynamic current loads that accelerate battery degradation. This work presents an end to end framework for motion aware battery health assessment in quadrotors. We first design a wide range current sensing module to capture motion specific current profiles during real flights, preserving transient features. In parallel, a high fidelity battery model is calibrated using reference performance tests and a metaheuristic based on a degradation coupled electrochemical model.By simulating measured flight loads in the calibrated model, we systematically resolve how different flight motions translate into degradation modes loss of lithium inventory and loss of active material as well as internal side reactions. The results demonstrate that even when two flight profiles consume the same average energy, their transient load structures can drive different degradation pathways, emphasizing the need for motion-aware battery management that balances efficiency with battery degradation.

</details>


### [297] [FLUX: Accelerating Cross-Embodiment Generative Navigation Policies via Rectified Flow and Static-to-Dynamic Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12806)
*Zeying Gong,Yangyi Zhong,Yiyi Ding,Tianshuai Hu,Guoyang Zhao,Lingdong Kong,Rong Li,Jiadi You,Junwei Liang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces DynBench for evaluating navigation tasks and proposes FLUX, a unified navigation policy that shows significant efficiency improvements and state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fragmented evaluation methods in navigation and enable comprehensive benchmarking for static and dynamic tasks.

Method: The study introduces DynBench for evaluating navigation tasks and develops FLUX, leveraging flow-based linearization and reinforcement learning to enhance navigation efficiency and adaptability.

Result: FLUX improves inference efficiency by 47% over prior flow-based methods, achieves state-of-the-art performance, and performs well in zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across various robot platforms.

Conclusion: DynBench offers a standardized framework for evaluating navigation tasks, and FLUX demonstrates the potential of unified navigation policies for comprehensive, efficient navigation performance.

Abstract: Autonomous navigation requires a broad spectrum of skills, from static goal-reaching to dynamic social traversal, yet evaluation remains fragmented across disparate protocols. We introduce DynBench, a dynamic navigation benchmark featuring physically valid crowd simulation. Combined with existing static protocols, it supports comprehensive evaluation across six fundamental navigation tasks. Within this framework, we propose FLUX, the first flow-based unified navigation policy. By linearizing probability flow, FLUX replaces iterative denoising with straight-line trajectories, improving per-step inference efficiency by 47% over prior flow-based methods and 29% over diffusion-based ones. Following a static-to-dynamic curriculum, FLUX initially establishes geometric priors and is subsequently refined through reinforcement learning in dynamic social environments. This regime not only strengthens socially-aware navigation but also enhances static task robustness by capturing recovery behaviors through stochastic action distributions. FLUX achieves state-of-the-art performance across all tasks and demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on wheeled, quadrupedal, and humanoid platforms without any fine-tuning.

</details>


### [298] [Reinforcement Learning for Elliptical Cylinder Motion Control Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12807)
*Pawel Marczewski,Paulina Superczynska,Jakub Bernat,Szymon Szczesny*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper addresses controlling an elliptic cylinder with limited torque using Reinforcement Learning (RL). A classical policy combining energy-shaping swing-up and LQR is used for comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the control problem of devices with limited input torque, specifically inspired by untethered magnetic devices that operate under such constraints.

Method: Reinforcement Learning (RL) was utilized to solve the control problem, with a two-stage classical controller (swing-up + LQR) used as a performance baseline.

Result: The RL-based solution demonstrates feasibility, though control challenges arise under specific conditions like stabilization with increased mass or more unequal ellipse perimeter ratios.

Conclusion: The study highlights both the potential and challenges in RL-based solutions, validating the approach but emphasizing its limits in complex scenarios.

Abstract: The control of devices with limited input always bring attention to solve by research due to its difficulty and non-trival solution. For instance, the inverted pendulum is benchmarking problem in control theory and machine learning. In this work, we are focused on the elliptical cylinder and its motion under limited torque. The inspiration of the problem is from untethered magnetic devices, which due to distance have to operate with limited input torque. In this work, the main goal is to define the control problem of elliptic cylinder with limited input torque and solve it by Reinforcement Learning. As a classical baseline, we evaluate a two-stage controller composed of an energy-shaping swing-up law and a local Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) stabilizer around the target equilibrium. The swing-up controller increases the system's mechanical energy to drive the state toward a neighborhood of the desired equilibrium, a linearization of the nonlinear model yields an LQR that regulates the angle and angular-rate states to the target orientation with bounded input. This swing-up + LQR policy is a strong, interpretable reference for underactuated system and serves a point of comparison to the learned policy under identical limits and parameters. The solution shows that the learning is possible however, the different cases like stabilization in upward position or rotating of half turn are very difficult for increasing mass or ellipses with a strongly unequal perimeter ratio.

</details>


### [299] [SmoothTurn: Learning to Turn Smoothly for Agile Navigation with Quadrupedal Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12842)
*Zunzhi You,Haolan Guo,Yunke Wang,Chang Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Quadrupedal robots often struggle to navigate sequential goals smoothly at high speeds. This paper introduces 'SmoothTurn,' a framework to optimize agility by enhancing smooth turning capabilities during rapid sequential navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current quadrupedal robot navigation policies prioritize single-goal reaching, which hampers seamless transitions and efficient motion during sequential goal navigation.

Method: SmoothTurn incorporates a sequential goal-reaching reward, expands observation space using a lookahead window, and employs an automatic goal curriculum to enhance agility in navigating sequential goals.

Result: Empirical analyses in simulations and real-world settings demonstrate that 'SmoothTurn' enables the robot to anticipate, plan, and execute efficient paths with smooth transitions between sequential goals.

Conclusion: The framework effectively advances agile locomotion for quadrupedal robots, resolving prior limitations in maintaining momentum and handling direction changes during sequential navigation tasks.

Abstract: Quadrupedal robots show great potential for valuable real-world applications such as fire rescue and industrial inspection. Such applications often require urgency and the ability to navigate agilely, which in turn demands the capability to change directions smoothly while running in high speed. Existing approaches for agile navigation typically learn a single-goal reaching policy by encouraging the robot to stay at the target position after reaching there. As a result, when the policy is used to reach sequential goals that require changing directions, it cannot anticipate upcoming maneuvers or maintain momentum across the switch of goals, thereby preventing the robot from fully exploiting its agility potential. In this work, we formulate the task as sequential local navigation, extending the single-goal-conditioned local navigation formulation in prior work. We then introduce SmoothTurn, a learning-based control framework that learns to turn smoothly while running rapidly for agile sequential local navigation. The framework adopts a novel sequential goal-reaching reward, an expanded observation space with a lookahead window for future goals, and an automatic goal curriculum that progressively expands the difficulty of sampled goal sequences based on the goal-reaching performance. The trained policy can be directly deployed on real quadrupedal robots with onboard sensors and computation. Both simulation and real-world empirical results show that SmoothTurn learns an agile locomotion policy that performs smooth turning across goals, with emergent behaviors such as controlling momentum when switching goals, facing towards the future goal in advance, and planning efficient paths. We have provided video demos of the learned motions in the supplementary materials. The source code and trained policies will be made available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [300] [Beyond Imitation: Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning for Adaptive Diffusion Navigation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12868)
*Junhe Sheng,Ruofei Bai,Kuan Xu,Ruimeng Liu,Jie Chen,Shenghai Yuan,Wei-Yun Yau,Lihua Xie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel reinforcement learning fine-tuning framework for diffusion-based robot navigation policies, addressing issues in unseen environments and improving generalization and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based robot navigation policies suffer performance degradation in unseen environments due to limited offline dataset coverage and difficulties in reinforcement learning adaptation.
